hso_hl.objective: Role-specific competence is established through a function-specific depth, audience-routing, competence-criteria-anchored training, and governance hooks architecture.
considerations: 5 of 5 regulations participate (GDPR, NIS 2, CRA, DORA, AI Act ŌĆö all 5 participate).

GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) `monitor compliance with this Regulation, including the assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations` ŌĆö DPO-catalysed role-specific training for processing staff with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities, on the controller's processing-staff audience.

NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 `offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices` ŌĆö Member-State-encouraged employee training on risk-identification capability (binding force on the Member State rather than directly on the entity per SR-NIS2-030).

CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) `where the product is intended for integration into other products, the information necessary for the integrator to comply with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Part I of Annex I and the documentation requirements set out in Annex VII` ŌĆö integrator-facing B2B information for Annex I compliance by the integrator ŌĆö **partial coverage** per AEGIS taxonomy ┬¦4.1.
DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit of their functions` ŌĆö financial-entity function-specific-depth training, on the financial-entity workforce with function-specific depth audience.

AI Act Art. 14(4)(a)ŌĆō(e) 5-element closed-list (properly understand; remain aware of automation bias; correctly interpret; decide not to use / disregard / override / reverse; intervene on operation or interrupt) + Art. 26(2) `competence, training AND authority AND support` 4-element AND ŌĆö AI Act human-overseer 5-element + 4-element competence set, on the high-risk-AI human-overseer audience within the deployer organization (provider-side Art. 14 design + deployer-side Art. 26(2) assignment).

10 pairwise cells in 5├Ś5 matrix: 5 verified by CRDA as SAME (3 SAME ŌĆö COMPLEMENTARY: GDPRŌåöNIS2, NIS2ŌåöDORA, NIS2ŌåöAI_Act; 1 SAME ŌĆö EQUAL: NIS2ŌåöDORA on workforce awareness at entity level; 1 SAME ŌĆö DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE: GDPRŌåöAI_Act on same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer); 5 CRDA-omitted pairs with Executor-recommended classifications (4 SCOPE-DISJOINT on different-audiences: GDPRŌåöCRA, NIS2ŌåöCRA, CRAŌåöDORA, CRAŌåöAI_Act ŌĆö workforce / encouraged-employee / financial-entity-workforce / AI-human-overseer vs B2B integrator; 1 COMPLEMENTARY: GDPRŌåöDORA ŌĆö DORA programme as carrier + GDPR role-specific module on DORA financial entity = controller).

The high-level SO engages on the Y-pairs integrated stack when Ōēź2 regs are applicable: GDPRŌåöNIS2 (Conditional Y on controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement); GDPRŌåöDORA (Y on DORA financial entity = controller with role-specific staff ŌĆö DORA programme as carrier + GDPR role-specific module); GDPRŌåöAI_Act (Conditional Y on same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer); NIS2ŌåöDORA (Y on DORA financial entity = NSIE applying role-specific training ŌĆö DORA-dominant for financial entities); NIS2ŌåöAI_Act (Y on NSIE deploys high-risk AI and assigns human oversight ŌĆö NIS 2 risk-identification baseline + AI Act 5-element competence stack); DORAŌåöAI_Act (Y on DORA financial entity deploys high-risk AI ŌĆö DORA `commensurate to the remit` carrier + AI Act 5-element sub-component).

The 4 CRA-involving pairs operate on substantively scope-disjoint audiences (internal workforce / Member-State-encouraged employee / financial-entity workforce / AI human overseer vs B2B integrator) ŌĆö the parallel tracks coexist with no consolidation rule; the high-level SO does NOT collapse the audience boundaries.

For a 5-FTE SaaS startup with GDPR + CRA applicability, the two parallel training-programme tracks must be maintained separately (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö GDPR workforce role-specific training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information).
The per-reg target role, trigger, anchor, threshold, evidence template, recipient are preserved verbatim in the five sub-SOs.
hso_hl.activation: auto  # activates when Ōēź2 applicable regs have Ōēź1 pair with Y scope overlap on the same party AND Ōēź1 sub-SO activates; for D-08.2 the 5 Y-pairs (GDPRŌåöNIS2 conditional, GDPRŌåöDORA recommended, GDPRŌåöAI_Act conditional, NIS2ŌåöDORA, NIS2ŌåöAI_Act, DORAŌåöAI_Act) cover all 5 participating regulators; the 4 N-pairs (GDPRŌåöCRA, NIS2ŌåöCRA, CRAŌåöDORA, CRAŌåöAI_Act) are substantively scope-disjoint on different audiences; once Ōēź2 regs are applicable the high-level SO engages on the joint-condition operator on the Y-pairs integrated stack

hso_per_reg.objective: The data protection officer monitors compliance with this Regulation, including the assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations (Art. 39(1)(b) GDPR) ŌĆö the role-specific layer of the DPO-catalysed awareness function.
considerations: The role-specific dimension targets staff with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities ŌĆö distinct from the general awareness programme at D-08.1.

The chosen reading of `awareness-raising` (per SR-GDPR-042 ambiguity_notes) splits the obligation into two: (1) general awareness training on GDPR for all personnel involved in processing, on an annual basis (D-08.1 layer); (2) role-specific training for personnel with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities, on a quarterly or event-triggered basis (D-08.2 layer).

PR.AT-02 anchors the role-specific layer ŌĆö personnel with data-subject-facing, access-management, breach-response or DPO-deputy responsibilities need role-specific reinforcement of their GDPR and cybersecurity obligations, distinct from the general awareness programme.

The role-specific depth is documented under Art. 5(2) accountability on the controller side ŌĆö the controller must demonstrate that the Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training was operationally exercised and recorded on the day of any supervisory inspection.

For an integrated controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement, the GDPR role-specific layer stacks with the NIS 2 Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training on the same workforce (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöNIS2 verified relationship).

For a DORA financial entity that is a controller processing personal data with role-specific staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training is the module (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöDORA Executor-recommended COMPLEMENTARY classification).

For an AI Act deployer-controller with the same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer, the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) layer is the additional competence dimension on top of the GDPR role-specific training (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a controller, the GDPR role-specific workforce training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö workforce vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöCRA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

hso_per_reg.activation: if NIS2 in applicable_regs  # entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the Member-State encouragement (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes)
hso_per_reg.objective: Member States shall encourage essential and important entities to offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices (Art. 20(2) sentence 2 NIS 2).
considerations: The binding force of Art. 20(2) sentence 2 is on the Member State rather than directly on the entity (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes) ŌĆö the entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the encouragement.

The cross-link to Art. 20(2) sentence 1 anchors the purpose clause: training must equip employees to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices, the same purpose clause that governs the management-body training in SR-NIS2-031.

The chosen reading of `shall encourage` is the cumulative reading (national policy + financial incentives + sectoral guidance + all) since the OJ is silent on the encouragement mechanism (per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes).

The compound `similar training` admits a parallel-to-management-training reading ŌĆö the chosen reading is literal (parallel-to-management-training because the OJ coordinates sentence 2 with sentence 1 within the same paragraph).
The temporal anchor `on a regular basis` is entity-discretionary under Recital 56.
PR.AT-01 anchors the recurring training content (phishing, social engineering, password discipline, threat-recognition capability ŌĆö per SR-NIS2-030 security_rationale).

PR.AT-02 anchors the role-specific dimension ŌĆö each employee understands how their own role contributes to the entity's cybersecurity objectives ŌĆö supporting the OJ's purpose clause that employees must `gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices`.

For an integrated controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement, the NIS 2 Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training stacks with the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training on the same workforce (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöNIS2 verified relationship).

For DORA financial entities, DORA-dominant for the role-specific training dimension (Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` function-specific calibration is more prescriptive than NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 encouraged-not-mandatory ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöDORA EQUAL classification).

For NSIEs deploying high-risk AI and assigning human oversight, the NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 employee risk-identification training stacks with the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also an NSIE, the NIS 2 employee risk-identification training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö workforce vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöCRA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

hso_per_reg.inherits_from: SO-DORA-015 (partial via Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit`)
hso_per_reg.objective: Financial entities develop ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in their staff training schemes, with a level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions (Art. 13(6) DORA).
considerations: The role-specific layer of the Art. 13(6) workforce training duty is anchored on `commensurate to the remit of their functions` ŌĆö function-specific depth on the workforce (D-08.2 layer), distinct from the baseline awareness programme at D-08.1.
The chosen reading of `level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions` is function-specific calibration per the OJ literal formulation (per SR-DORA-025 ambiguity_notes).

**T4-vs-text gap awareness:** T4 reads `role-specific training` but the OJ Art. 13(6) uses only `commensurate to the remit of their functions` (per SR-DORA-025 ambiguity_notes); the Executor keeps the OJ-literal locus.

PR.AT-02 layers the role-specific depth dimension ŌĆö staff in security-critical roles (incident response, ICT operations, fraud investigation, third-party governance) receive calibrated depth rather than a uniform baseline. Together with PR.AT-01 (the baseline awareness layer at D-08.1), the two operationalise both the `compulsory` and the `commensurate` axes of Art. 13(6).

For an integrated financial-entity-controller with role-specific staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training is the module (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöDORA Executor-recommended COMPLEMENTARY classification).

For DORA financial entities that are also NSIEs, DORA-dominant for the role-specific training dimension (Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` function-specific calibration is more prescriptive than NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 encouraged-not-mandatory ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöDORA EQUAL classification).

For DORA financial entities deploying high-risk AI, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence is the sub-component (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 DORAŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a DORA financial entity, the DORA Art. 13(6) internal staff training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö internal staff vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 CRAŌåöDORA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

hso_per_reg.objective: High-risk AI systems are designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use (Art. 14(1) AI Act) ŌĆö the provider-side design obligation for the human-oversight capability.
considerations: The natural persons assigned to human oversight are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate, to discharge the 5 cumulative abilities of Art. 14(4): (a) properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system and foreseeable misuse; (b) remain aware of automation bias; (c) correctly interpret the output; (d) decide not to use the high-risk AI system or to disregard, override or reverse its output; (e) intervene on the operation or interrupt the system through a stop button or similar procedure.
The 5-element closed list is AND-coordinated (all five must be enabled cumulatively ŌĆö partial compliance is non-compliance per SR-AIACT-013 ambiguity_notes).

On the deployer-side assignment obligation, deployers of high-risk AI systems assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support (Art. 26(2) AI Act) ŌĆö the 4-element AND `competence, training AND authority AND support` (all four required per SR-AIACT-020 ambiguity_notes).
The provider-design-by-design + deployer-assignment-on-deployment twin-trigger is the AI-Act-specific competence architecture.
PR.AT-02 anchors the role-understanding dimension ŌĆö the workforce-supplier analog of `properly understand` (Art. 14(4)(a)) and `correctly interpret` (Art. 14(4)(c)).

PR.AT-01 anchors the awareness-and-training substrate ŌĆö `remain aware of automation bias` (Art. 14(4)(b)) + `decide` (Art. 14(4)(d)) + `intervene` (Art. 14(4)(e)) require overseers to detect and respond to AI-specific failure modes including the cognitive-bias correction that `automation bias` operationalises.
GV.RR-02 supplies the RACI substrate ŌĆö overseers must hold formal authority to override and interrupt the system.

For an integrated GDPR-controller deployer with the same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer, the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) layer is the additional competence dimension on top of the GDPR role-specific training (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For NSIEs deploying high-risk AI and assigning human oversight, the NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 employee risk-identification training stacks with the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For DORA financial entities deploying high-risk AI, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence is the sub-component (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 DORAŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).

For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also an AI Act provider, the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information is independent of the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer competence (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö B2B integrator vs AI-overseer role within deployer organization ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 CRAŌåöAI_Act Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

cross_reg_analysis.raw_md:
## 1. Cross-Regulation Analysis

> **Domain analysis:** [D-08.2 Role-Specific Competence](../../DomainAnalysis/D-08_Human-Factors/D-08.2.md)

### D-08.2 Role-Specific Competence

<!-- participants: GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA, AI_Act -->

**Participants (from CRDA):** GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA, AI_Act

**Sole-authority / partial-coverage notes:** CRA's role-specific dimension is integrator-facing (Annex II ┬¦8(f)) ŌĆö B2B audience, not workforce. The asymmetry (workforce training for NIS 2 / DORA / GDPR vs. product-integrator training for CRA) is unique to D-08.2.

#### Pair: GDPR Ōåö NIS2

**GDPR article (verbatim, Art. 39(1)(b)):**
> "The data protection officer shall ŌĆ” monitor compliance with this Regulation, including the assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations ŌĆ”" ŌĆö Art. 39(1)(b) GDPR

**NIS2 article (verbatim, Art. 20(2) sentence 2):**
> "Member States shall encourage essential and important entities to offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices." ŌĆö Art. 20(2) sentence 2 NIS 2

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: DPO-driven role-specific training for staff with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities.
- NIS 2 scope: Member-State-encouraged (not mandatory) employee training on risk identification and assessment.
- Scope overlap: **Conditional (Y when the controller-NSIE trains staff with both data-protection and risk-identification responsibilities).**

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: DPO-catalysed role-specific training.
- NIS 2 trigger: Member-State encouragement.
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT** (DPO-direct vs. encouraged-indirect).

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: no quantitative cadence.
- NIS 2: `on a regular basis` ŌĆö entity-discretionary under Recital 56.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: data subject, DPA.
- NIS 2: management body, competent authority.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: DPO-discretionary curriculum.
- NIS 2: not codified for content (entity-discretionary).

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME ŌĆö COMPLEMENTARY**
**Reasoning:** GDPR role-specific is DPO-catalysed and tied to processing role; NIS 2 employee training is encouraged and tied to risk-identification capability. Different content (data-protection vs. risk-identification) and different governance hooks (DPO vs. encouraged). The Art. 20(2) sentence 2 binding force is on the Member State rather than directly on the entity (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030).

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **Conditional (Y when the controller-NSIE adopts the encouragement; N otherwise)**. No reclassification required.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce a layered role-specific training stack: GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed (entity-side obligation) + NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 encouraged (entity-side, conditional on MS encouragement adoption).

---

#### Pair: GDPR Ōåö CRA

> **Note:** CRDA did not analyze this pair for D-08.2. CRDA lists 5 pairs only (GDPRŌåöNIS2, GDPRŌåöAI_Act, NIS2ŌåöDORA, NIS2ŌåöAI_Act, DORAŌåöAI_Act); the GDPRŌåöCRA pair was not included.

**GDPR article (verbatim, Art. 39(1)(b)):**
> [as quoted above]

**CRA article (verbatim, Annex II ┬¦8(f)):**
> "The product shall be accompanied by detailed instructions and information ŌĆ” including ŌĆ” (f) where the product is intended for integration into other products, the information necessary for the integrator to comply with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Part I of Annex I and the documentation requirements set out in Annex VII." ŌĆö CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f)

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: DPO-driven role-specific training for staff involved in processing.
- CRA scope: integrator-facing information (B2B) for Annex I compliance by the integrator.
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** ŌĆö different audiences (workforce vs. integrator-B2B), different objects (data-protection role training vs. Annex I compliance documentation).

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: DPO-catalysed role-specific training.
- CRA trigger: manufacturer ships integrator-facing information with product.
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT**.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: no quantitative cadence.
- CRA: support period + 10-year Annex II accessibility.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: data subject, DPA.
- CRA: integrator (B2B), MSA.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: DPO-discretionary curriculum.
- CRA: Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator information.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** NOT CLASSIFIED (CRDA omitted this pair for D-08.2)
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SCOPE-DISJOINT** (recommended)
**Reasoning:** The two regimes address entirely different audiences and objects ŌĆö GDPR workforce training (DPO-catalysed) vs. CRA integrator-facing information (manufacturer-shipped). The Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator audience is a B2B external party, not an internal workforce member. There is no overlap in actors or objects; the substantive classification is scope-disjoint.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **N** ŌĆö different audiences (workforce vs. integrator-B2B), different objects, different triggers. Substantively scope-disjoint.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce two parallel tracks: GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) workforce role-specific + CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information. CRDA's omission should be corrected by adding a "scope-disjoint" classification.

---

#### Pair: GDPR Ōåö DORA

> **Note:** CRDA did not explicitly analyze this pair for D-08.2 (CRDA lists 5 pairs only).

### D-08.2.1 ŌĆö Sub-SO for GDPR

```yaml
id: SO-D-08.2.GDPR
subdomain: D-08.2
applies_to: [GDPR]
inherits_from: SO-GDPR-025 (cross-ref at D-08.2)
source_SR: SR-GDPR-042
activation: if GDPR in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: COMPLEMENTARY (CRDA-deep, D-08.2: GDPRŌåöNIS2 = COMPLEMENTARY (SAME ŌĆö Conditional Y on controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement); GDPRŌåöDORA = COMPLEMENTARY (recommended ŌĆö DORA programme as carrier + GDPR role-specific module on DORA financial entity = controller); GDPRŌåöAI_Act = DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE (SAME ŌĆö Conditional Y on same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer); GDPRŌåöCRA = SCOPE-DISJOINT (recommended ŌĆö workforce vs integrator-B2B audiences; different objects))
```

**Objective.** The data protection officer monitors compliance with this Regulation, including the assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations (Art. 39(1)(b) GDPR) ŌĆö the role-specific layer of the DPO-catalysed awareness function.

**Considerations.**
- The role-specific dimension targets staff with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities ŌĆö distinct from the general awareness programme at D-08.1.
- The chosen reading of `awareness-raising` (per SR-GDPR-042 ambiguity_notes) splits the obligation into two: (1) general awareness training on GDPR for all personnel involved in processing, on an annual basis (D-08.1 layer); (2) role-specific training for personnel with data-subject-facing or access-management responsibilities, on a quarterly or event-triggered basis (D-08.2 layer).
- PR.AT-02 anchors the role-specific layer ŌĆö personnel with data-subject-facing, access-management, breach-response or DPO-deputy responsibilities need role-specific reinforcement of their GDPR and cybersecurity obligations, distinct from the general awareness programme.
- The role-specific depth is documented under Art. 5(2) accountability on the controller side ŌĆö the controller must demonstrate that the Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training was operationally exercised and recorded on the day of any supervisory inspection.
- For an integrated controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement, the GDPR role-specific layer stacks with the NIS 2 Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training on the same workforce (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöNIS2 verified relationship).
- For a DORA financial entity that is a controller processing personal data with role-specific staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training is the module (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöDORA Executor-recommended COMPLEMENTARY classification).
- For an AI Act deployer-controller with the same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer, the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) layer is the additional competence dimension on top of the GDPR role-specific training (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a controller, the GDPR role-specific workforce training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö workforce vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöCRA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-GDPR-042` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.AT-01, PR.AT-02.
*(PR.AT-01 captures the broad-coverage discipline (D-08.1 layer) ŌĆö the DPO's awareness-raising obligation is structurally a general-population training duty covering all personnel involved in processing, on a recurring cadence and with content that updates to reflect emerging threats and the changing data flows of the organisation. PR.AT-02 captures the role-specific layer (D-08.2 layer) that PR.AT-01 cannot cover: personnel with data-subject-facing, access-management, breach-response or DPO-deputy responsibilities need role-specific reinforcement of their GDPR and cybersecurity obligations, distinct from the general awareness programme. The two subcategories together enable the controller or processor to demonstrate ex post, against the Art. 5(2) accountability duty read with the Art. 39(1) DPO mandate, that the DPO's awareness-and-training function ŌĆö both the general-awareness layer and the role-specific layer ŌĆö was operationally exercised and recorded on the day of any supervisory inspection.)*

### D-08.2.2 ŌĆö Sub-SO for NIS 2

```yaml

subdomain: D-08.2
applies_to: [NIS2]

source_SR: SR-NIS2-030
activation: if NIS2 in applicable_regs  # entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the Member-State encouragement (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes)
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: COMPLEMENTARY (CRDA-deep, D-08.2: GDPRŌåöNIS2 = COMPLEMENTARY (SAME ŌĆö Conditional Y on controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement); NIS2ŌåöDORA = EQUAL (SAME ŌĆö both regulations impose role-specific training; DORA-dominant for financial entities); NIS2ŌåöAI_Act = COMPLEMENTARY (SAME ŌĆö NIS 2 risk-identification baseline + AI Act 5-element competence on NSIE deploying high-risk AI); NIS2ŌåöCRA = SCOPE-DISJOINT (recommended ŌĆö workforce vs integrator-B2B audiences; different objects))
```

**Objective.** Member States shall encourage essential and important entities to offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices (Art. 20(2) sentence 2 NIS 2).

**Considerations.**
- The binding force of Art. 20(2) sentence 2 is on the Member State rather than directly on the entity (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes) ŌĆö the entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the encouragement.
- The cross-link to Art. 20(2) sentence 1 anchors the purpose clause: training must equip employees to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices, the same purpose clause that governs the management-body training in SR-NIS2-031.
- The chosen reading of `shall encourage` is the cumulative reading (national policy + financial incentives + sectoral guidance + all) since the OJ is silent on the encouragement mechanism (per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes).
- The compound `similar training` admits a parallel-to-management-training reading ŌĆö the chosen reading is literal (parallel-to-management-training because the OJ coordinates sentence 2 with sentence 1 within the same paragraph).
- The temporal anchor `on a regular basis` is entity-discretionary under Recital 56.
- PR.AT-01 anchors the recurring training content (phishing, social engineering, password discipline, threat-recognition capability ŌĆö per SR-NIS2-030 security_rationale).
- PR.AT-02 anchors the role-specific dimension ŌĆö each employee understands how their own role contributes to the entity's cybersecurity objectives ŌĆö supporting the OJ's purpose clause that employees must `gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices`.
- For an integrated controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement, the NIS 2 Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training stacks with the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training on the same workforce (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöNIS2 verified relationship).
- For DORA financial entities, DORA-dominant for the role-specific training dimension (Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` function-specific calibration is more prescriptive than NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 encouraged-not-mandatory ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöDORA EQUAL classification).
- For NSIEs deploying high-risk AI and assigning human oversight, the NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 employee risk-identification training stacks with the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also an NSIE, the NIS 2 employee risk-identification training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö workforce vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöCRA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-NIS2-030` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.AT-01, PR.AT-02.
*(PR.AT-01 anchors the recurring training content: phishing, social engineering, password discipline, and the threat-recognition capability that the OJ's purpose clause requires employees to possess. PR.AT-02 anchors the role-specific dimension: each employee understands how their own role contributes to the entity's cybersecurity objectives, supporting the OJ's purpose clause that employees must `gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices`. Together, PR.AT-01 and PR.AT-02 operationalise the workforce-literacy substrate. The control set enables ex-post demonstration that the OJ's `regular basis` anchor is discharged against a documented training curriculum anchored in the NIST NICE Workforce Framework and proportionate to the entity's exposure.)*

### D-08.2.3 ŌĆö Sub-SO for CRA (partial)

**Objective.** Where the product with digital elements is intended for integration into other products with digital elements, the manufacturer provides the information necessary for the integrator to comply with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Part I of Annex I and the documentation requirements set out in Annex VII (Annex II ┬¦8(f) CRA).

PR.AT-04, GV.SC-03.
*(PR.AT-04 anchors the integrator's understanding of the upstream product's threat model, configuration constraints, and vulnerability-handling interface ŌĆö the workforce-supplier analog for the integrator side. GV.SC-03 anchors the contractual / information-disclosure layer: the ┬¦8(f) information is the information counterpart to a contractually-anchored SCRM commitment. ### D-08.2.4 ŌĆö Sub-SO for DORA

```yaml
id: SO-D-08.2.DORA
subdomain: D-08.2
applies_to: [DORA]
inherits_from: SO-DORA-015 (partial via Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit`)
source_SR: SR-DORA-025
activation: if DORA in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: COMPLEMENTARY (CRDA-deep, D-08.2: NIS2ŌåöDORA = EQUAL (SAME ŌĆö both regulations impose role-specific training; DORA-dominant for financial entities); DORAŌåöAI_Act = COMPLEMENTARY (SAME ŌĆö DORA `commensurate to the remit` carrier + AI Act 5-element sub-component on DORA financial entity deploying high-risk AI); GDPRŌåöDORA = COMPLEMENTARY (recommended ŌĆö DORA programme as carrier + GDPR role-specific module on DORA financial entity = controller); CRAŌåöDORA = SCOPE-DISJOINT (recommended ŌĆö integrator-B2B vs internal staff audiences; different objects))
```

**Objective.** Financial entities develop ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in their staff training schemes, with a level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions (Art. 13(6) DORA).

**Considerations.**
- The role-specific layer of the Art. 13(6) workforce training duty is anchored on `commensurate to the remit of their functions` ŌĆö function-specific depth on the workforce (D-08.2 layer), distinct from the baseline awareness programme at D-08.1.
- The chosen reading of `level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions` is function-specific calibration per the OJ literal formulation (per SR-DORA-025 ambiguity_notes).
- **T4-vs-text gap awareness:** T4 reads `role-specific training` but the OJ Art. 13(6) uses only `commensurate to the remit of their functions` (per SR-DORA-025 ambiguity_notes); the Executor keeps the OJ-literal locus.
- PR.AT-02 layers the role-specific depth dimension ŌĆö staff in security-critical roles (incident response, ICT operations, fraud investigation, third-party governance) receive calibrated depth rather than a uniform baseline. Together with PR.AT-01 (the baseline awareness layer at D-08.1), the two operationalise both the `compulsory` and the `commensurate` axes of Art. 13(6).
- For an integrated financial-entity-controller with role-specific staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training is the module (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöDORA Executor-recommended COMPLEMENTARY classification).
- For DORA financial entities that are also NSIEs, DORA-dominant for the role-specific training dimension (Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` function-specific calibration is more prescriptive than NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 encouraged-not-mandatory ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöDORA EQUAL classification).
- For DORA financial entities deploying high-risk AI, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence is the sub-component (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 DORAŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a DORA financial entity, the DORA Art. 13(6) internal staff training is independent of the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö internal staff vs B2B integrator ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 CRAŌåöDORA Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-DORA-025` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.AT-01, PR.AT-02.
*(PR.AT-01 anchors the baseline awareness layer that closes the human-factor gap ŌĆö phishing recognition, social-engineering resistance, password hygiene, incident-reporting reflexes ŌĆö and converts the OJ `ICT security awareness programmes` from an opt-in continuing-professional-development activity into a compulsory training-module floor across the workforce (D-08.1 layer). PR.AT-02 layers the role-specific depth dimension that the OJ `commensurate to the remit of their functions` qualifier demands (D-08.2 layer), ensuring that staff in security-critical roles (incident response, ICT operations, fraud investigation, third-party governance) receive calibrated depth rather than a uniform baseline. Together these two subcategories operationalise both the `compulsory` and the `commensurate` axes of Art. 13(6), and an entity evidencing PR.AT-01 coverage plus PR.AT-02 role-calibrated depth can demonstrate ex-post to supervisory review that the Art. 13(6) workforce-training obligation was met at both the universal-awareness floor (D-08.1) and the role-specific depth required by the staff function taxonomy (D-08.2), and that completion thresholds were documented as a condition of employment rather than as optional professional development.)*

**Objective.** High-risk AI systems are designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use (Art. 14(1) AI Act) ŌĆö the provider-side design obligation for the human-oversight capability.

**Considerations.**
- The natural persons assigned to human oversight are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate, to discharge the 5 cumulative abilities of Art. 14(4): (a) properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system and foreseeable misuse; (b) remain aware of automation bias; (c) correctly interpret the output; (d) decide not to use the high-risk AI system or to disregard, override or reverse its output; (e) intervene on the operation or interrupt the system through a stop button or similar procedure.
- The 5-element closed list is AND-coordinated (all five must be enabled cumulatively ŌĆö partial compliance is non-compliance per SR-AIACT-013 ambiguity_notes).
- On the deployer-side assignment obligation, deployers of high-risk AI systems assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support (Art. 26(2) AI Act) ŌĆö the 4-element AND `competence, training AND authority AND support` (all four required per SR-AIACT-020 ambiguity_notes).
- The provider-design-by-design + deployer-assignment-on-deployment twin-trigger is the AI-Act-specific competence architecture.
- PR.AT-02 anchors the role-understanding dimension ŌĆö the workforce-supplier analog of `properly understand` (Art. 14(4)(a)) and `correctly interpret` (Art. 14(4)(c)).
- PR.AT-01 anchors the awareness-and-training substrate ŌĆö `remain aware of automation bias` (Art. 14(4)(b)) + `decide` (Art. 14(4)(d)) + `intervene` (Art. 14(4)(e)) require overseers to detect and respond to AI-specific failure modes including the cognitive-bias correction that `automation bias` operationalises.
- GV.RR-02 supplies the RACI substrate ŌĆö overseers must hold formal authority to override and interrupt the system.
- For an integrated GDPR-controller deployer with the same natural person as both data-processing staff and AI human overseer, the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) layer is the additional competence dimension on top of the GDPR role-specific training (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 GDPRŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For NSIEs deploying high-risk AI and assigning human oversight, the NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 employee risk-identification training stacks with the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 NIS2ŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For DORA financial entities deploying high-risk AI, the DORA Art. 13(6) `commensurate to the remit` programme is the carrier and the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence is the sub-component (per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 DORAŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
- For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also an AI Act provider, the CRA Annex II ┬¦8(f) integrator-facing information is independent of the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer competence (substantively scope-disjoint audiences ŌĆö B2B integrator vs AI-overseer role within deployer organization ŌĆö per CRDA-deep ┬¦D-08.2 CRAŌåöAI_Act Executor-recommended SCOPE-DISJOINT classification ŌĆö no consolidation rule applies).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-AIACT-013` v0.3 + `SR-AIACT-020` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.AT-02, PR.AT-01, GV.RR-02.
*(PR.AT-02 covers the role-understanding dimension that sub-points (a) `properly understand` and (c) `correctly interpret` require. PR.AT-01 anchors the awareness-and-training substrate that sub-point (b) `remain aware of automation bias` and sub-points (d) `decide` and (e) `intervene` require, ensuring overseers detect and respond to AI-specific failure modes including the cognitive-bias correction that `automation bias` operationalises. GV.RR-02 supplies the RACI substrate that sub-points (d) and (e) require ŌĆö overseers must hold formal authority to override and interrupt the system. A provider evidencing PR.AT-02 role understanding, PR.AT-01 awareness training and GV.RR-02 RACI definition demonstrates ex post that the five-element Art. 14(4) oversight-abilities list ŌĆö cumulative AND of all five abilities plus the last-resort sub-point (e) intervention safety net ŌĆö is operationalised in the provider's training and tooling programme. The Art. 26(2) deployer-side 4-element AND `competence, training AND authority AND support` adds the deployer's personnel-qualification anchor (PR.AT-02) + the deployer's formal RACI definition (GV.RR-02) + the deployer's HR-practices integration (GV.RR-04 ŌĆö supplementary).)*

### 8.2.2 ŌĆö Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training on a regular basis

```yaml

  volere_subtype: immunity

the temporal anchor `on a regular basis` is entity-discretionary under Recital 56 (annual, biannual, ad-hoc, or event-triggered cadence ŌĆö entity choice); the compound `similar training` reads as parallel-to-management-training per SR-NIS2-030 (the OJ coordinates sentence 2 with sentence 1 within the same paragraph); the chosen reading of `shall encourage` is the cumulative reading (national policy + financial incentives + sectoral guidance + all) since the OJ is silent on the encouragement mechanism; the entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the encouragement (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ŌĆö the Article 20(2) sentence 2 binding force is on the Member State rather than directly on the entity); the training is anchored in the NIST NICE Workforce Framework and proportionate to the entity's exposure; for an integrated controller-NSIE adopting Member-State encouragement, the NIS 2 Member-State-encouraged employee risk-identification training stacks with the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed role-specific training on the same workforce (Conditional Y per CRDA-deep ┬¦8.2 GDPRŌåöNIS2 verified relationship ŌĆö joint curriculum on the Y-pair);   applicable_if:

    scope_overlap: Conditional   # NIS2ŌåöGDPR = Y (Cond on controller-NSIE adopting encouragement); NIS2ŌåöDORA = Y (DORA financial entity = NSIE; DORA-dominant on role-specific training); NIS2ŌåöAI_Act = Y (NSIE deploys high-risk AI and assigns human oversight); NIS2ŌåöCRA = N substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs integrator-B2B); HL engages on overlap

  considerations: |
    The NIS 2 role-specific competence obligation is the **Member-State-encouraged employee training on risk-identification and assessment capability** ŌĆö Art. 20(2) sentence 2 `offer similar training to their employees on a regular basis, in order that they gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices`. The binding force of Art. 20(2) sentence 2 is on the Member State rather than directly on the entity (Orchestrator-recommended treatment per SR-NIS2-030 ambiguity_notes) ŌĆö the entity-side SR applies to entities that adopt the encouragement. The cross-link to Art. 20(2) sentence 1 anchors the purpose clause: training must equip employees to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices, the same purpose clause that governs the management-body training in SR-NIS2-031 (cross to 8.3 layer). The chosen reading of `shall encourage` is the cumulative reading (national policy + financial incentives + sectoral guidance + all) since the OJ is silent on the encouragement mechanism. For NSIEs deploying high-risk AI and assigning human oversight, the NIS 2 Art. 20(2) sentence 2 employee risk-identification training stacks with the AI Act Art. 14(4) + Art. 26(2) human-overseer 5-element + 4-element AND competence (per CRDA-deep ┬¦8.2 NIS2ŌåöAI_Act verified relationship).
```
